description: A10FJ - Day 1, part 6: Housekeeping and TV transmission

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 5 hours, 38 minutes into the Mission.
Apollo 10 is 25,033 miles [46,361 km] from the Earth.
Velocity; 12,157 feet per second [3,706 metres per second].
Glynn Lunney and the Black Team of flight controllers getting ready to hand over to Flight Director Milton Windler and the Gold Team at this time.
I beg your pardon, that is the Maroon Team.
Milton Windler and the Maroon Team.
There have been a few brief conversations on air to ground.
We'll play those for you now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're maneuvering Automaneuver to an attitude to start Delta P23.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: To make optical star/Earth and or star/Moon navigational measurements, the crew are using P23 to automaneuver to the correct attitude to take the required sightings, whilst in P23 manual optics mode.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Looks like our RCS fuel budget's in real good shape.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We concur on the RCS fuel budget.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You all have already loaded that W-matrix for us?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: W-matrix-- error transition matrix used in the CMC Navigation Updating Program (P23).
See page fromMission Techniques Contingency Procedures.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It had the right hook-in numbers in there, I just don't know if the option is right.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We loaded that in erasable and should be good.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: There will be no midcourse correction number 1.

We're going to delay until the nominal time of midcourse correction number 2 to start with the midcourses, and instead of just passing you the TLI plus 11 PAD at this time, we'd like to pass you the TLI plus 11, the plus 25, and the plus 35-hour PAD.
The last one will be valid under the assumption that we don't have a midcourse correction 2 and we'll update it after midcourse correction number 2.

For your information, the Delta-V of midcourse correction 2 at 26 hours and 33 minutes will be about 48.9 feet per second which is only about 2 feet per second larger than we would require for a midcourse correction number 1 at the nominal time.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: It sounds like a real great idea.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: If you're ready to copy, I've got three P37 PADs to pass.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Looks like the S-IVB did a good job for us and also what we calculated on that separation burn.
Put us right down the money.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: P37 block data for TLI plus 11 hours: 013:30, 5,201, minus 165, 046:37.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: TLI plus 35 hour PAD: 037:30, 5,037, minus 165, 094:35, Read back.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Readback is correct and for your information, although you are now in a free return trajectory, your entry angle is very steep, currently about 65 degrees.
In the event of lost comm, you could correct this with P37.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The purpose of P37 (program 37) on this occasion is to compute a return-to-Earth trajectory providing the CSM is outside the lunar sphere of influence at the time of ignition.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Details of the P37 PAD for TLI plus 11 hours are:GETIGround Elapsed Time of ignition:13 hours, 30 minutes.Delta-VT:5,201 fps.
This is the total velocity change that would be experienced by the spacecraft.Longitude:165° West.
This is the longitude of the Earth landing point for entry guidance.GET 400k:46 hours, 37 minutes.

Ground Elapsed Time of Entry Interface.Details of the P37 PAD for TLI plus 25 hours are:GETI:27 hours, 30 minutes.Delta-VT:5,795 fps.Longitude:165° West.GET 400k:70 hours, 28 minutes.Details of the P37 PAD for TLI plus 35 hours are:GETI:37 hours, 30 minutes.Delta-VT:5,037 fps.Longitude:165° West.GET 400k:94 hours, 35 minutes.Typical P37 PADP37 is only suitable for aborts that would occur well within Earth's sphere of influence.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is querying the projected very steep re-entry angle 65°.
The usual angle is 6.5° ±1° to the horizontal when the CM reaches the Entry Interface altitude (400,000 ft, 121.92 km).
The crew would be able to compute an appropriate return trajectory using program 37 using the above PAD's for the required burn time which would bring them back to a safe re-entry corridor angle.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: From the Apollo 10 Mission Report, August 1969: - The Translunar Injection maneuver was performed with excellent results.
The resulting pericynthion altitude solution was 907.7 miles, as compared with the preflight prediction of 956.8 miles.
This altitude difference is consistent with a 0.5-ft/sec accuracy in the injection maneuver.
Upon completion of circumlunar flight, Earth capture of the spacecraft would have been assured, since the uncorrected flight-path angle at entry was minus 64.24 degrees.
The Service Module Reaction Control System could easily have adjusted these entry conditions to acceptable values if the service propulsion system had failed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 6 hours, 3 minutes into the flight of Apollo 10.
At the present time, our spacecraft velocity is 11,602 feet per second [3,537 metres per second] and are now at an altitude of 27,546 nautical miles [51,015 km] from Earth.

Here at Mission Control, Plant Director Milton Windler and his team of flight controllers have taken over from Glynn Lunney we have a change of shift Press briefing scheduled in the auditorium of building 1 in about 5 minutes from now.
We've had some brief conversation with the crew since the previous announcement.

The crew is advised that the midcourse correction nominally scheduled to take place at 11 hours, 33 minutes will be postponed until the second opportunity which will be about 26 hours, 30 hours Ground Elapsed Time.

The reason for postponing the midcourse correction until the second opportunity is the fact that the change in velocity required will be very little greater for the second opportunity and this will lessen the chance that further midcourse corrections will be needed on route to the Moon.
At the present time, we'll play back the conversations we've had with the crew and then stand by for any further communication.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Should I go ahead and turn my H2purge line heater off; I may have forgotten it.
I've still got it on now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Purging of the fuel cells is a function of power demand and gas purity (O2& H2).
O2purging requires 2 minutes and H2purging, 80 seconds.
A hydrogen purge is preceded by activation of the H2Purge Line Heater (Main Display Console 3) 20 minutes prior to the purge.
The fuel cell purge cycle is determined by the mission power profile and gas purity as sampled after the spacecraft cryogenic tank fills.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Go ahead and turn off your H2purge line heater, and under our revised schedule of passing PADs up, we're not currently planning to send you any PADs at GET of 12 hours.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We'd like you to do an optics calibration at the end of this P23.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Whilst still in P23 by entering V59 (Verb 59) the CMC will carry out a calibration of the optics subsystem, using one of the preset stars as a reference.
The optics calibration is know as routine R57.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1969 Technical debrief:"Optics calibration was extremely difficult because there were not any visible stars to fly to in the telescope.
So we put it off as long as we could.
On the way to the Moon, there was never a case where we had more than one or two stars visible, even 180 degrees from the Sun.

There was no place where we could recognize constellations on the way to the Moon, and there were very few places, where we were doing P23's or P23-type things, where we could recognize individual stars except through the optics.
It would be very convenient if there were a routine in the computer which would fly the spacecraft to position a star for optics calibration if they're required for each P23.
It would save you a great deal of fuel, especially when you can't see the darned stars in the first place.
When you were doing star/landmark tracking in the vicinity of the Earth, you had to do the optics calibration to find a star a great deal further away from the Earth than the procedure recommends.
The procedure wants you to do the optics calibration in the vicinity of the body that you're tracking.
The Earth, the Moon, and the Sun cause star shafting across the telescope, and completely blank your vision from seeing any stars with the Lunar Module on.
The Lunar Module caused Sun shafting into the optics at various positions.
From the start of TLI through translunar coast, we were never at any time able to see any stars except through Auto optics."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: From theApollo 10 Mission Report:"Star/Earth-horizon measurements were made to determine the bias calibration for horizon altitude required to execute the return-to-Earth navigation program in the event of a communication loss.

The sightings were easy to perform with automatic positioning of the optics; however, Earthlight "banding" in the telescope optics hindered visual acquisition of a star in the vicinity of the Earth, such as when conducting a trunnion bias check.
Fortunately, a sextant search in the vicinity of Mars located Antares, and the trunnion checks could be completed.

Because of the difficulty in locating a star for bias-calibration check in the vicinity of bright bodies, automatic maneuvers to the star/landmark line-of-sight axis should be incorporated into future star/navigation computer programs."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I can't see any of them out here much.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And that was the end of it, near as I can figure.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're recommending that you use star 33, Antares, for the optics Cal.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We know you can see that one.
And 10, is it your intention after you finish with this to return to TV?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We'll give you another look as soon as we can.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Could you give us an attitude - sort of a PTC attitude that would enable us to - to see the Earth?
I'd sure - We'd sure appreciate it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're working on an attitude now.
I don't believe that the TV and the PTC are compatible.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The Passive Thermal Control (PTC) mode will make good communications for TV transmission problematic, due the constant change in attitude.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Give us an attitude so we can make an Automaneuver to show on the TV after we finish here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'll have that for you in a minute or so.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We can hand-hold the camera out the side hatch, and it's compatible with the PTC, don't you think?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: On the TV, we're working toward using the camera out the right-hand window.
Although the hatch window would be compatible with PTC, we can't keep your High Gain Antenna lock all the time during PTC.
And, did you power down the BMAG's?
We show both of them Off.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We'll have to look at that.
We're going to hand over from Goldstone to Hawaii at 6 hours, 30 minutes GET.
The TV will still be through Goldstone.
And, have you had your VHF on since TLI?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We're supposed to turn it off at 7 hours.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We've been in VHF Simplex since about Tananarive.[see GET 000:39:13]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We could really do great star landmark on Baja California.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Point Conception is clear as a bell.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: For TV coverage, put the Earth in the right-hand window.
We recommend you roll to 263 degrees and hold your present pitch and yaw.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And in the previous conversation, I asked you about the BMAG status.
We had erroneous TM [telemetry] indications down here.
It looks like you're in good shape.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Los Angeles looks clear today except for a little smog, I believe.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Houston, what was that roll angle again?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Just wanted to check one thing on the SPS burn.
I show 90 psi on my thrust chamber pressure indicator.
I just wondered how that correlated with telemetry, and what do you think of the engine.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The SPS thrust chamber pressure (SPS PC) has a normal range of 95 to 105% (psia).
The SPS PCis indicated in a dial on panel 1.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It's going to take us about 5 minutes to dig out the data for correlation on the chamber pressure and the SPS status.
When you called that down previously, right after the evasive maneuver, I didn't hear any groans down here.
People seemed to think it was fairly good and...
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We were looking for about 100 to 105 psi.
We know it's a single bank, and would probably be a little less, but we're looking for close to - looking for 100, but we know that a guage error could be close to that.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'll get you a good correlation from the data.
And, did you all successfully complete the optics calibration?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Takes a long time to get to Antares, Bruce.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We estimate that it may be several minutes before the crew has completed activities and is in a position for television transmission.
We don't have an accurate time estimate on how long that will be, but we'll continue to stand by.
At the present time the spacecraft velocity is continuing to drop off 10,739 feet per second [3,274 metres per second], and our altitude is 32,135 nautical miles [59,514 km] from Earth.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Further analysis shows that that roll 263 degree angle we gave you is not compatible with the High Gain Antenna.
You're going to have to put the camera out the left-hand window, and we're working on a new attitude for you for that.
And, would you verify that your attitude set switch is in the GDC [Gyro Display Coupler] position?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We could go to GDC if you want.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Would you, go to GDC, please?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The Att Set switch on panel 1 has two positions.IMU:Total attitude signals from IMU are routed to resolvers in Attitude Set panel to generate Euler errors.

These error signals can be displayed on FDAI error needles if selected.GDC:Same concept as for IMU position except total attitude signals are obtained from GDC and Euler errors are transferred to body errors in GDC.This latter position is necessary for GDC alignment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The best, guess is that that trunnion calibration is 89995.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Now what number do you want us to load into Noun 87?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: As part of the optics calibration routine in P23, the trunnion bias refers to the declination axis of the spacecraft's optical systems, the sextant and the scanning telescope.
It is measured using V06N87 (Verb 06, Noun 87).
It is displayed on register 2 of the DSKY (Display and keyboard) as xx.xxx degrees.
Young is reading off this angle as 89.995 degrees.
He is now querying whether he has to load the difference between this angle and 90 degrees, i.e.
He is corrected that he does not have to enter any number, just push the Proceed button on the DSKY.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: There's no need to load a number in.
Just hit Proceed and go from where you are.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: That's what we did, Bruce; and, of course, we're all done, so that will be good for the next time we do star-horizon.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We loaded a zero in there when we first started this thing, which probably accounts for some of the update.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Do you have a new attitude you want us to go to?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Your TV attitude is roll 023 degrees, pitch 181 [degrees], and yaw 041 [degrees].
This gives you High Gain Antenna angles of pitch plus 28 degrees, yaw 307 [degrees].
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Roll 02300 [degrees], pitch is 181 [degrees], yaw is 041[degrees]; pitch is plus 28 [degrees], and yaw is minus 07 [degrees].
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The High Gain Antenna is pitch plus 28 [degrees], yaw 307 [degrees].
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're starting maneuver to that attitude.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: On telemetry, we showed 95 psi for SPS chamber pressure during the burn, and that is about right for one-bank operation.
All the data that we've got down here looks nominal.
The SPS is looking very good.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I knew there could be some instrument error, I just wanted to correlate.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're going to the TV attitude now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And, 10, if you'd be interested there's just a possibility of a waste-water dump during TV.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We could substitute another kind if you want to.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: McCandless is likely referring to the humorous possibility of the crew carrying out a urine dump during the TV transmission.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: On your High Gain for the TV pass, we suggest that you go from wide beamwidth to medium due to your increasing distance from the Earth.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And I suddenly have it over here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll give high gain a try now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 7 hours, 6 minutes.
We're continuing to stand by for an unscheduled television transmission from the spacecraft and waiting for the crew to get the spacecraft in the proper attitude – the antennas properly located.
At the present time, our altitude is 34,099 nautical miles [63,151 km] from Earth.
We're traveling at a speed of 10,428 feet per second [3,179 metres per second], and we'll continue to stand by.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: In fact, I can now see the Hawaiian Islands.
The subsolar point is right over the Hawaiian Islands.
You can see them from here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We haven't got you on TV yet.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: It looks real good on the monitor.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We've got the Earth now in the black and white; it will be about a 10-second delay for color.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You're filling up about 80 per cent of the screen vertically.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Again, it's kind of an awkward position to hold it, but again, you can barely see Baja California and Mexico real well.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: If you could roll the camera right or left 90 degrees and then zoom a little more, we could fill up nearly the whole screen.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And we've got the North Pole on the right of our screen down here, and the Atlantic Ocean was the terminator at the bottom of the screen.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And either you have clouds over the Sierra Nevada's or they're snowcaps at this time.
I can't tell which from here.
You can still see the San Joaquin Valley.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Houston, we're going to zoom in on it a little bit; and, also, we're deactivating the VHF [VHF Simplex] at this time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Deactivating the VHF and zooming in.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You can see that subsolar point very well in this picture.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We can see the very bright spot on the surface of the water that is the subsolar point.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: It looks silvery, about the same color as the clouds here, only obviously brighter.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You can see night-time coming over on the eastern part of the United States, too.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And while you're doing this, we'd like to uplink you a PTC REFSMMAT.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The new REFSMMAT the ground wish to uplink is the new frame of reference which suits operations during the translunar coast, particularly the need to regularly rotate the spacecraft side-on to the Sun.
This PTC (Passive Thermal Control) REFSMMAT is based on the plane of the ecliptic and the ecliptic poles.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The platform's X-axis will now be aligned along the ecliptic plane, perpendicular to the line between the Earth and Moon at the time of TLI.
its Z-axis will be aligned perpendicular to the ecliptic plane, directed southward.
This alignment is used for all translunar and trans-Earth coast manoeuvres and as the reference for easy alignment of the PTC (Passive Thermal Control) manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Can you describe for us the area that the northern clouds seem to be obscuring?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: They start up in the Northwest Territories of Canada and actually ring out to Alaska, and from there they go down just about to the Canadian - United States border and go on east.
But the whole northwest Pacific, across northern Canada and over to Greenland is all obscured with just a solid white mass of clouds as you can see in your - near the North Pole.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We can see the California coastline and Baja California down in the lower right-hand corner of our screen.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I've zoomed down a little bit.
Now you can really notice those clouds that cover about the northern - looks about the northern third or quarter of the Earth there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Up in the vicinity of Alaska, we see a swirl.
Does that look like a storm system or low pressure area to you?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You've got a swirl out there right on the - off the coast of Alaska.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: How are the colors coming through down there, Bruce?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Oh, the colors are coming through beautifully.
The oceans are a beautiful blue-green.
We can see the land masses in a brown to reddish-brown.
The vicinity of the North Pole, the clouds and ice caps seems to be saturating a little; but on the whole, it's all coming through nicely.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And you can - The area right east of the Sierra Nevada's, now - I guess around the Rockies - as night-time starts to spread over the United States, is becoming more of a purplish-red.
You can see Texas, Oklahoma, and that area; it's becoming more of a purplish-red, and the rest of it is still a bright red - a bright red to brown.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We can see the terminator quite clearly moving up from lower right-hand corner of our screen.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: It's really amazing the cloud cover we have here and the swirls that you can see through.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We had a computer problem here that delayed our REFSMMAT uplink.
We're ready to go now, though.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You all planning any interior shots this pass?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We can turn on the floodlights for just a minute here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We are going to go around and shoot an interior now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: As soon as we get all arranged around here, we'll show you a quick inside shot.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You can go back to Block.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: When in Block, no uplink data can be sent to the CMC.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, you're coming through on our black and white monitor now very well.
We'll see the color in a minute.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: John is just sitting upside-down here in the LEB [Lower Equipment Bay].
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We see Smiling John down there.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: He's just turning around down here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Tom, you can't believe - it's really great.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We're looking into the LEB now, and looking at John Young on the right.
You need to focus a little when you get in closer.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Are you in the interior position on the camera?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We're looking at Gene right now.
Understand you all haven't got your suits off yet.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: John has his suit off and all stowed, and Gene and I don't.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We got a good look at the DSKY a few seconds ago as you panned past it.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We see your pack on the screen right now.
Can zoom in on that a little?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: That's coming through loud and clear, now.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: That's the best I can do.
I'll try to focus it closer.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's the one on the front cover of your Flight Plan, I believe.
We can read the writing on the Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: It's really coming through beautifully down here.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We see your name plate; there's Stafford.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: That's how you can tell who it is, huh?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That's how we can tell who it is.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You can see the sun coming in over my shoulder, and then on Tom, it's really tremendous.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: For information, all these are at f:22.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: In fact, that's what we shot the Earth at, was f:22.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Looks like you got some suntan yesterday, Tom.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I have to stay healthy there, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'll take it back over here at left window and show you Earth again.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We've got the terminator to our left, and it looks like the South Pole on the top of the screen.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We're going to have a communications handover at about 7 hours, 30 minutes GET, or about 4 hours from now, And the black and white is really looking good.
The color is showing a little bit of saturation on the white.
Could you tell us the position of your ALC [Automatic Light Compensation] switch?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We're showing the orientation reversed from what you had it a few minutes ago.
Is it convenient for you to turn the world upside-down or right-side-up here?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Houston, I was just wondering if this target that I'm tracking out here in the sextant might be the S-IVB, by any chance?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Can you give us some angles on it?
Maybe we can do something with that?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You can see the terminator really starting to move across the United States, now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Would you say the terminator is about over the Rocky Mountains now?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Now it's starting to get dark.
You can see the shadows from the Rocky Mountains out on the plains in eastern Colorado and New Mexico, but it's darker more over toward Louisiana and the east part of Texas.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: This picture is coming to us from an altitude of about 36,300 nautical miles [67,227 km].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: On the perimeter of the Earth on our monitors, we're showing a few little bulges that look like they're in the scanning equipment - the horizontal scan on the monitor that stays fixed in position on the monitor as the image moves up and down.
Do you have these on your monitor?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We lost you during that switchover for a minute.
I think we got you again.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Reading you loud and clear now.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: What was it you said about bulges?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: On our monitor down here, both black and white and color, we're showing a little bulge that looks like it's in the horizontal sweep system, and we were wondering whether this indentation in the surface of the Earth as it appears on your monitor was present, or whether it's in our equipment.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We see it on our monitor, too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're going to have to terminate the TV now, and stand by to get some other gear squared away.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Would you be interested in showing a water dump?
We're having some problems with the waste-water transducer and we're interested in dumping down to zero to verify the transducer.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Are we Go for a waste-water dump?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'd like to count you down on the dumping and monitor at our TM as well as on board.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I thought you only wanted to do these things before a maneuver.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Well, we've got midcourse correction number 1 which we canceled.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We were going to go ahead and do this at about 10 or 11 hours anyway to verify the transducer.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: How soon do you want to start the waste-water dump, Houston?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It'll be ready in about 2 or 3 minutes, Tom.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Are you getting some bright spots on your clouds?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're getting what looks like glare coming off of a window or perhaps glare off the lens - sort of a thin mask over the view of the Earth.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Well, I tell you what, we're going to go ahead and turn it off now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Why don't you go ahead and run your P52 through the PTC REFSMMAT now, and we'll get the waste-water dump when you're through with that.

We'll have a Flight Plan update for you here shortly, with an eye toward clearing the way for you to commence your sleep period or rest period early, if you so desire; and, if you'd be working on any questions you may have or problems, for us that we can work while things are quiet here, we'll he standing by to receive them.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We're realigning right now to the PTC REFSMMAT, and we're going to Gyro Torque.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Using P52, option code 00003 on register 2 of the DSKY, the crew are realigning the IMU to the newly received PTC REFSMMAT, torquing the IMU gimbals to correspond with the new REFSMMAT.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control At 7 hours, 41 minutes, and at the present time the spacecraft is at an altitude of 37,321 nautical miles [69,118 km] and the velocity has now dropped down below 10,000 feet per second [3,000 metres per second] and is currently reading 9,935 feet per second [3,029 metres per second].

During the previous transmission, you heard the crew advised that because of the fact that they won't be doing the mid-course correction at the time scheduled originally at 11 hours, 33, it will be possible, if they desire, for them to begin their rest period a little early, We will continue to stand by for any further conversation, bring the circuit up in the event we hear from the crew further.
This is Apollo Control at 7 hours, 42 minutes into the flight of Apollo 10.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, I have been looking at about 39, maybe 39.2 volts on this battery for about the last 20 minutes.
Do you want me to keep going to 39.5?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Keep charging until you get to 39.5.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The batteries in the CM are recharged via the SM fuel cells.
There are 5 silver oxide/zinc batteries located in the CM lower equipment bay.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Three rechargeable entry and postlanding batteries (A, B, and C) power the CM systems after CSM separation and during postlanding.
Prior to CSM separation, the batteries provide a secondary source of power while the fuel cells are the primary source.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Two pyrotechnic batteries supply power to initiate ordnance devices in the spacecraft.
The pyrotechnic batteries are isolated from the rest of the EPS to prevent the high-power surges in the pyrotechnic system from affecting the EPS, and to ensure source power when required.
These batteries are not to be recharged in flight.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is querying to what level he should charge battery A.
The batteries are rated at 37.8 volts when open circuit.
When charging, the measured voltage rises as the batteries near full charge until a charging voltage of 39.5 volts is reached, at which point the charging is terminated.
The three rechargeable batteries are rated to 40 ampere hours (AH) each.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I've got the waste-water dump procedure here.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You can go ahead with the procedures.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We would like you, when you start to dump until the quantity indication stops decreasing, and then continue dumping for 5 minutes or until you get a Go from us to discontinue dumping.
We would also like to get a Mark from you when you start the dump.
Your onboard read-out will probably stop decreasing prior to a zero indication due to the instrumentation calibration.
And we will be ready to start this whenever you are through with P52 and it is convenient with you.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Now you want us to do a waste-water dump until the quantity stops, and then for 5 minutes or until you give us the GO.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We are ready to go ahead with the wastewater dump at any time here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The contents of the waste water tank are dumped overboard to space via the water pressure relief valve.
The H2O Quantity Indicator switch is set to Waste on panel 2.
On panel 352 the Pressure Relief valve is set to Dump A.
The Waste H2O Quantity indicator on panel 2 is monitored until the required reduction is observed.
The Pressure Relief valve is then returned to the Off position.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We are showing a slow decrease on our TM data.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: It's our intention to secure the TV lines down here unless you desire otherwise.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We're all finished here for today.
We got some other things we have to take care of.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Did most of the color look pretty good there on your monitor, Bruce?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Oh, it really looked beautiful, Tom.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Snoopy has been awful quiet out there.
How is he looking to you?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The only parameter we can see from the LM is the current through the translunar bus tie.
We have no reason to believe that Snoopy is anything but nominal, though.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: He's in his normal, relaxed configuration.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: CM - LM/CM Delta-P gauge is now down to five-tenths, for some reason.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Your LM/CM Delta-P down to five-tenths of a psi.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I guess that goes hand-in-hand with what our cabin pressure is.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: What does your waste water show?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We are showing about 20.5 per cent.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Could you give us a hack on what your waste water is indicating up there, please?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Waste water is indicating now 20 per cent.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It's about what you said, right?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Now it shows about 18 per cent.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We are showing about 5 per cent less than you, down here.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Do you want to give us a hack when you want us to stop it?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: During that series of conversations, you heard Tom Stafford advise that he had no further plans for television today.
Here in the Control Center, we have had a change of capsule communicators.
Astronaut Joe Engle has relieved CapCom Bruce McCandless.
A short while ago, the spacecraft just crossed the 40,000 nautical miles [74,080 km] marker.

Current altitude reading is 40,156 nautical miles [74,369 km] and the speed is 9,556 feet per second [2,913 metres per second], This is Apollo Control at 8 hours, 11 minutes into the flight of Apollo 10.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Could you give us a hack when your waste-water quantity stops decreasing?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: We're showing just about zero, and that's just about the same difference we've been running all along.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Appears that the waste water quantity has stopped decreasing.
It's showing about 4 to 5 percent, as close as I can read the gauge.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Do you want us to terminate the dump?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We want to hold for about 5 minutes here, Tom.
And we'll give you a call when to turn it off.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: You can terminate that waste-water dump now if you want to.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And, 10, we've got an update for your erasable memory table here, a few new numbers for you.
And we've also got a Flight Plan update for you when you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: We're kind of busy right now, Joe, taking suits off and scrambling around in here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Just give us a call when you've got some time.
However, we would like to get into that PTC as soon as is convenient for you, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: John's already realigned to the REFSMMAT.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Go ahead with any updates you've got here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I guess the first thing is this erasable memory table.
I've got three new numbers for you on that for 03, 4, and 5 in column B.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: For row 3, 03, the number is 0012, row 04, the number is 13256; and for row 05, the number is 33266.
And that's all for that one.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: These figure are for manual entery into the CMC erasable memory table should an uplink of this data fail.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I got some Flight Plan update items here if you're ready to note them down now.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: First off we're going to delete all the midcourse 1 burn data, of course.
We'd like for you to set up the PTC as soon as it's convenient for you, and we'd like the FDAI scale select to 50/15.
We'd like to monitor that deadband a little closer.
And we'd like for you to go ahead and perform on your schedule the battery vent, and if you could, we'd like to have the manifold pressure before and after the battery vent.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: The FDAI attitude error needles indicate the difference between the actual and desired spacecraft attitude.
The attitude error signal can be derived from several sources; The uncaged BMAG's from GA-1, the CDU's (PGNCS), or the ASCP-GDC/IMU.
Positive attitude error is indicated by a downward displacement of the pitch error needle, and by a leftward displacement of the yaw and roll error needles.
The attitude error needle displacements are "fly-to" indications as related to rotation control direction of motion, required to reduce the error to zero.
The ranges of the error needles are ± 5 degrees or ± 50 degrees for full-scale roll error, and ± 5 degrees or ± l5 degrees for pitch and yaw error.
The error scale factors are selected by the FDAI SCALE switch on panel 1 that also establishes the rate scales.
The pitch and yaw attitude error scales contain graduation marks at null and ±full scale, and at ±1/3 and ±2/3 of full scale.
The roll attitude scale contains marks at null, ±½, and ± full scale.
The attitude error indicators utilize servometric meter movements.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The entry and postlanding batteries pressure is relieved as specified in the Flight Plan after completion of cabin purge by positioning the control (on RHLEB panel 252) to VENT for 5 seconds.
After completion the control is closed, and pressure as read out on position 4A of the System Test Meter (LEB panel 10l) should remain at zero unless there is battery outgassing.
This outgassing can be caused by an internal battery failure, an abnormal high-rate discharge, or by overcharging.
If a pressure increase is noted on the system test meter, the Battery Vent is positioned to Vent for 5 seconds, and reclosed.
Normal battery charging procedures require a check of the battery manifold after completion of recharge.

Since the battery vent line is connected to the waste water dump line, it provides a means of monitoring waste water dump line plugging, which would be indicated by a pressure rise in the battery manifold line when the Battery Vent control is positioned to Vent.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, again on your schedule, fuel cell O2purge; and, also, canister A change; and, finally, terminate the cabin purge.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: What time do you want the cabin purge terminated?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'll go ahead with that cabin purge on your checklist the way it is on your schedule right now in your Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: 10, coming back at you on that cabin purge, you can do that whenever it is convenient for you - whenever it looks good - whenever you have the time, then.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: One thing we've noticed here, Joe, is that the O2Flow High light keeps coming on, and we're running pretty high.
I guess we can expect this until we close the vent, right?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: As the CM O2supplied is being used to pressurize the LM, the O2flow rate is high until the crew close the LM tunnel vent.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flow rates of 1 pound per hour or more with a duration of 16.5 ± 1.5 seconds will illuminate the O2FLOW HI light on the caution and warning panel (panel 2).
The warning is intended to alert the crew to the fact that the oxygen flow rate is greater than is normally required.
It does not necessarily mean that a malfunction has occurred, since there are a number of flight operations in which a high-oxygen flow rate is normal.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The cabin pressure regulator controls the flow of oxygen into the cabin to make up for depletion of the gas due to metabolic consumption, normal leakage, or for repressurization.
The assembly consists of two absolute pressure regulators operating in parallel, and a manually operated Cabin Repress valve.
The regulator is designed to maintain cabin pressure at 5 ± 0.2 psia at flow rates up to 1.4 pounds per hour.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Tom, we'd like for you to continue that battery charge, also, as long as we can.
We'll terminate that just prior to your sleep period.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And, I guess two more items is all.
We're going to delete the P37 PAD: TLI plus 44 and TLI plus 53, and that will be updated postsleep, after your sleep.
And you can perform your pre-sleep checklist and start your rest period whenever you want to.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: We still - It takes quite a while to get everything reconfigured around here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're just getting around to eating now, too.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Have you had any problem servicing those bags out of that food preparation spout, Tom?
Does the water keep coming out at all after you pull the bag off?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Manifold read 1.55 on the 4A test meter before, and 0.60 afterwards.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 8 hours, 35 minutes.
The current altitude now is 41,067 feet [means nautical miles] [76,056 km] and our velocity is 9,447 feet per second [2,880 metres per second].
We're presently in conversation with the spacecraft.
We also have some accumulated tape.
we'll pick up the tape and then continue to follow whatever live conversation develops.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Can we hold on about another 30 or 40 minutes before going to the PTC REFSMMAT?
We want to get this food squared away.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're never going to get up to 39½ volts.
I'm still looking at about 39.2 in that Battery A charger, but I'll leave it on if you want me to.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'd like to leave it on just as long as we can, and try to get it on up there to 39.5 or so, and we'll take it off before you go to sleep, though; we'll remind you.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 9 hours, 5 minutes into the flight of Apollo 10.
The spacecraft is currently at an altitude of 44,838 nautical miles [83,040 km] and the speed is about 9,000 feet per second [2,744 metres per second].
It's been relatively quiet both here in mission control and from the spacecraft since our previous report.
This period of time is set aside for the crew to have their evening meal, following that they'll have some housekeeping activities aboard the spacecraft getting set for a rest period.
The crew has been advised that they can begin their rest period somewhat early because of the deletion of the first, midcourse correction.
That midcourse correction, has been postponed until the second opportunity at about 26 hours, 30 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We do have a brief amount of tape.
We'll play that for you now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 009:21:41 Comm Tech:Goldstone, Houston Comm Tech, Net 1.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 009:21:44 Comm Tech:This is Goldstone Comm Tech.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 009:21:47 Comm Tech:I hear you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stand by to monitor CapCom's transmissions.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'd like to feed up a NAV update to you if you could give us P00 and Accept; we could do that while you're eating.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: We're eating, and we're finishing getting out of suits and cleaning up.
You've got P00 and here comes Accept.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 009:27:35 Comm Tech:Goldstone, Houston Comm Tech, conference.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 009:27:45 Comm Tech:Goldstone, Houston Comm Tech, conference.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 009:27:53 Comm Tech:Voice control, Houston Comm Tech, conference.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 009:28:30 Comm Tech:Goldstone, Houston Comm Tech, conference.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 009:28:43 Comm Tech:I hear you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: George, give me a turn at the computer and I'll go ahead and start my O2purge.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I didn't want to call and bother you; I thought you were still eating there.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 9 hours, 31 minutes.
At this time, the flight of Apollo 10 continues to run very smoothly.
The spacecraft is now 47,034 nautical miles [87,106 km] from Earth and the Command Service Module and the LM combined had a total weight of 94,027 pounds [42,747 kg].
It's continued to be rather quiet, both here in Mission Control and conversation with the spacecraft.
We do have about a minute to a minute and a half of tape we'll play back for you now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I'll start on fuel cell 3 on the O2.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Fuel cells require occasional purging to remove impurities from the electrodes.
The FC Ind (fuel cell indicator) is turned to select the fuel cell that is to be purged.
The FC Purge switch for the relevant fuel cell is placed in the O2position for 2 minutes.
The FC O2Flow indicator will display the O2flow rate.
O2flow rate will increase by 0.6 lb/hr.
This may cause FC O2Flow indicator to exceed its normal limits and activate Master Alarm push button/light and FC 1 (2 or 3) light.
Alarm limits may be checked by monitoring FC O2Flow indicator at the moment C/W light comes on.
The FC Purge switch is then returned to the Off position.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It appears we'll have no further conversation with the crew at this time.
As you heard, Gene Cernan mention they're finishing their evening meal at the present time and the Flight Plan shows that they will be doing a routine oxygen purge of their fuel cell.

This will be to remove any impurities which may have collected in the fuel cell during the course of the mission and they will also, within the next 30 minutes or so, be changing one of the lithium hydroxide canisters which remove carbon monoxide [means carbon dioxide] from the spacecraft atmosphere.
As we mentioned earlier, the first opportunity for midcourse correction, scheduled for 11 hours, 33 minutes into the mission, has been postponed.

Earlier in the day, the Flight Dynamics Officer reported that the midcourse correction, if done in 11 hours and 33 minutes would require a velocity change about 48 or correction about 47.3 feet per second [14.4 metres per second].

By delaying this midcourse until 26 hours 30 minutes at which time the second opportunity would come up, the correction delta velocity would only be about 1½ feet per second greater or about 48.9 feet per second.

And it was felt that by waiting this additional time would gain additional tracking information approve the knowledge of where the spacecraft was and perhaps reduce the possibility of needing further midcourse correctionsen routeto the Moon.
At 9 hours, 37 minutes, this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The fuel cell purge is - O2purge is done.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How are things going down there?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Boy, things are just looking real well down here, Gene.
You look like you're doing a real good job.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: How'd the lift-off look to you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, I'll tell you, babe, that S-IC is a real ride.
There's no question when you're light off and lift off the ground and then you go through Max-Q, and after that it's smooth as silk until you come near staging, and then all hell breaks loose.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Boy, you guys sure made one heck of a racket getting out of there.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: That's probably because of the east wind.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That wind was drifting and blowing in the right direction.
It really rattled the cages around there.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I would have liked to have seen the expression on Tracy's face on that one.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The T&D looked real good on the TV; it looked just like it does in the simulator.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I'm glad we were able to show it.
It worked out pretty good from here.
John did a real outstanding job of turning his vehicle around and plugging in.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: In fact, I thought there for a while you were just running a taped replay from the simulator.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: That's all it is, babe, isn't it?
They've just got a better visual for us.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You've got a good model up there to work with.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 10 hours, 2 minutes into the flight of Apollo 10.
And currently, the spacecraft is traveling at a speed of 8,507 feet per second [2,593 metres per second], and it's about 49,650 nautical miles [91,951 km] from Earth.
Since our previous report, we've had about 1 or 2 minutes of conversation with the spacecraft.
After finishing their evening meal, Gene Cernan had a brief conversation with flight controllers here in Mission Control concerning this morning's lift-off.
We'll play back that tape for you now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I think we're set up in the PTC REFSMMAT with a 20-degree deadband and around - about 275 degrees, or thereabout.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: From theApollo 10 Mission Report:"At approximately 10.5 hours, Passive Thermal Control was initiated using a 0.1 deg/sec roll rate and a 20-degree deadband about the other two axes.
This control configuration resulted in frequent reaction control thruster firings when the spacecraft drifted into the yaw and pitch deadbands.
Thruster firing produced a small vibration when the Lunar Module was attached that was readily noticeable by all three crewmen.
Damping of the vibration occurred in about three to four cycles.
Even with the above perturbations, the crew slept soundly the first night."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford, from the1969 Technical debrief:"The first night, when we set up the dead band, we noticed near the end, that when the vehicle would reach the deadband, the thruster would fire.
The noise of the thruster was not annoying, but the total dynamic response of the vehicle was.
You could feel about three or four cycles and it felt like a very loose vehicle - like a large airplane - something like a B-47 or a C-133, the way that the structural dynamics worked.
After that, the next night, we changed a procedure, that is damped the rates to zero before we set up the mode, instead of setting up the mode and then damping the rates.
After that the longest period of time we went without firing any thruster was 18 hours."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1969 Technical debrief:"It's recommended that this PTC setup and operation be considered a nominal PTC G&N mode, and that it be used in all future flights.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: to take a look at that, and see if it got - got all in there.
It's not always clear that some of these optical numbers get down in that thing.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Yes, we'll check it out, John.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: All that - All that REFSMMAT stuff looks good.
What we'd like to do is take over the antennas.
Next time you go to either Bravo or Delta, if you'd give us a call, we'll take over, then.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I'll give you the omnis at this time.
Stand by until I get you one.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'm on omni on A, but that's not what you want.
I can't get a good lock-on on B, right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: When you get to B, let us know.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Either - 10, this is Houston again - Either Bravo or Delta, let us know, and we'll try and work at it from this end.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Have you changed out that canister in the ECS [Environmental Control System] yet?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: The canister has just been changed.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Houston, I can fly on D right now; only one switch in D and the other in B, if you'd like.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Tell your friendly man on the left side there of you, Joe, that ...
CDR has taken one aspirin, CMP two, LMP two, and the LMP also has one Lomotil only entered in the log.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The friendly man on the left would have been the Flight Surgeon.
Each day the crew gave a crew health report which would include the amount of sleep, details of any medication taken, fluid intake, food consumed and PRD (Personnel radiation dosimeter) reading.
If required the crew could speak on a private circuit to report health related issues.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: When you get a chance, the man on the left would like to have PRD readouts for all three of you.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I think they're all stowed in the suits.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 10 hours, 32 minutes.
Apollo 10 now 51,847 nautical miles [96,020 km] from Earth and traveling at a speed of about 8,300 feet per second [2,530 metres per second].
A short while ago we heard from John Young aboard the spacecraft.
He advised that the crew has now started the Passive Thermal Control which is a slow rotation rate of the spacecraft to maintain proper thermal equilibrium.
At that rotational rate, it completes 1 revolution about every hour.
We'll play back the tape conversation that we've had with the crew and then stand by for any live communication.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: One thing we'd like to do - We're thinking about going to sack out now - is to go ahead and shut the waste vent there, and so we'll - We won't have any O2High Flow light.
And also, what time do you want to terminate the battery charge?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Entry and post-landing battery A has been charging and this is now being terminated.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You can go ahead and terminate that battery charge anytime now.
We have a few other things we're going to pass up to you, and let me make sure I get them all lined up here, and I'll give them all to you at once.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Battery charge coming Off, and the Batt relay bus circuit breaker coming In.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And we want to go ahead and shut that vent now so we won't - Our O2High Flow light ...
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You got an update to the Flight Plan or anything?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We don't have one right now, Tom.
We've got some other little things we're going to pass up to you here in just a minute.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: We're all prepared to sack out shortly.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'll have it to you here in about 2 or 3 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: We've got about six or seven things here, Tom.
First off, we notice that RHC number 2 power switch is still On, and we'd like to have that Off.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: The second thing, we'd like to advise you if you want to get a hold of us during the night anytime, the best mode is Downvoice Backup.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: The CM voice input is pre-emphasized, clipped, and routed directly to the S-band for PM transmission, bypassing the PMP voice modulator.
The PMP accomplishes signal modulation and demodulation and signal mixing.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The next item, Tom: we'd like to have the LM/CSM Delta-P pressure, if you can get that for us, please.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Request made for reading of the pressure differential between the CM cabin and the LM tunnel.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'll have that for you in a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're going to be watching this waste water H2O build-up during the night, and we'll keep monitoring that.
And we'd like to take over omni switching, and to do that, we'd like to have you go to High Gain, the High Gain Omni switch to Omni, if you would.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And we'd like to confirm - all - Roger.
And we'd like to confirm that the S-band antenna is in Omni and Bravo.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I guess the only other thing is we'd kind of like your comments on how that PTC is going, how it looks from up there.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: We've got all the window shades up, and we're just slowly rotating here, and you can tell the spacecraft started to cool down right away.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It feels real good in here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I said that's real interesting you could tell it cooling down right away.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Are there any questions that have come up, up there, that you'd like for us to work on tonight while you are resting?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: The only thing, we just - it was just because we probably haven't seen it in the simulator and forgot it in debriefings is that O2flow.
Like I'm reading 0.8 On the O2flow right now.
Is that supposed to be the normal flow?
Should be lots less than that, shouldn't it?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: EECOM says it'll take that a little while to come down to a lower - lower value.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: As far as we can see up here, all the systems just look real fine.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Let me clarify on switch setting here with Flight, Tom.
Give me about half a minute here, and I'll be right with you.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: The LM/CS - LM/CM Delta-P gauge is reading 0.6.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I guess about one more thing, or two more things.
One of them is: we just want to verify these antenna switches.
I may have confused you some on that.
On the S-band antenna, we want one in Omni and one in Bravo.
Is that - Is that where you had them?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: One's in Omni, and one's in Bravo.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The other thing I guess we need is the onboard readout for the battery.
That's for the Flight Plan there.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: We'll go ahead and get them for you.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Ready to call in the readings to you.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Pyro Battery C is 36, pardon me, this is Batt C is 36.8.
Pyro Batt B is 37.1, RCS A is 93.0, B is 93.0, C is 99.0, and D is 94.0.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The Flight Plan calls for onboard readouts of the CM rechargeable batteries charge levels and the SM RCS propellant quantities.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And it looks like we should be running well ahead of our RCS budget here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I guess the parting shot here.
Flight says all the consumables look real good.
Everything's looking real good for tonight.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Y'all have a good sleep, and we'll see you in the morning.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes, it sounds like shortly we'll soon be about 55,000 miles out, right?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Sounds like a long way from home, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You guys covered a lot of ground today.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I'll tell you, when that Saturn starts moving out, you can tell it's going to cover a lot of ground.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I would sure like to have seen that from the ground, too, Joe.
I bet that was when that big bear lifted off.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Boy, that was a beautiful sight.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Could you see us all the way through staging?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Yes, it - there was a little cloud deck.
You disappeared for a while, then you broke out into the open again, and there were - there were two decks actually that you went through, you could - You could track it a long, long way out.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: That wind was blowing just about the direction, you were talking about.
It really rattled the cages over there at the VAB.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I tell you, it rattled a few cages during TLI, too, around here.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That TLI frequency was a little bit too much.
We thought sure it was coming unglued.
It wasn't anything bad or anything, but just those oscillations, not pogos, but just vibrations.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Not quite as bad as the 104 when you throttle chop out in Mach 2 and ...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Tom, did that water taste - Could you taste any chlorine at all in that water when you first started using it?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: You bet your sweet bippy we could.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Has it - Has it gotten any better?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes, it's gotten lots better, but there was chlorine in it to start with.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That is a good theory, Joe, it just don't work.
But it's mighty good, though, it tastes like mighty good water right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, since we got off on time and when they serviced that water, do we have to chlorinate that stuff tonight?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The man on the left is talking.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: You're right about that probe; it worked.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Well, it's got to work one more time, John.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Then - Then I'll be around to collect.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Hey, on this chlorination, it looks like - it looks as per Flight Plan we'd probably better go ahead and chlorinate.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And - and it will - it will cycle.
If you chlorinate tonight, you'll get rid of a lot of that taste by the time you use it in the morning.
And I'd advise you drinking all you want before you chlorinate, though, and then - and then give it the shot and by morning it won't be quite as bad.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: But I thought they said if we got off on time we could probably go 2 days without it.
Is the Cape talking to Houston these days?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I don't know; I'll find out.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.Day 1, part 5: LM extraction and S-IVB separationJournal Home PageDay 1, part 7: PTC concerns and sleep
